metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Verification entry (and corresponding AC) covering the sign-time HEAD-mismatch path: what the system does when ticket_store_revision at lock time differs from the collected snapshot's revision.
evidence: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.'
Verification only lists: manifest parsing, relationship collection, re-sign parity, and legacy-reader compatibility — no sign-time revision mismatch/retry path.
findings.finding: The Implementation describes a sign-time retry/verification mechanism — 'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' after outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions — but the Verification section lists no test command or fixture covering what happens when the HEAD changes between collection and lock acquisition, leaving the retry/failure path untested.
findings.location: Implementation (phase story paragraph) vs. Verification section
scenarios: A concurrent write changes ticket HEAD between snapshot collection and sign lock acquisition; the retry or abort path is never exercised by tests, so the behavior is unverified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the closed `PlanRelationSnapshotError` reason-code vocabulary (e.g. as a `Literal` union or `enum.Enum`) co-located with the exception class definition, mapping each of the five triggering conditions (missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, canonical-ID-mismatch, ticket-store-read-failure) to a specific string constant.
evidence: Plan text: 'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, canonical-ID-mismatch, or ticket-store-read failures raise internal PlanRelationSnapshotError with a closed reason code and optional target reference.'
Plan text: 'Each PlanRelationSnapshotError reason produces a structured ERROR log without a traceback fallback.'
Plan text: 'Failure-path tests cover every ManifestFormatError variant and every PlanRelationSnapshotError reason'
ABSENCE: No enum/constant table or string literals for reason codes appear anywhere in the plan. An executor must invent the vocabulary, making test-coverage assertions non-deterministic.
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshotError reason codes are referenced as a 'closed' enum throughout the plan and AC/Verification, but their actual vocabulary is never defined. The Implementation enumerates the triggering conditions in prose (missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, canonical-ID-mismatch, ticket-store-read failures), yet never lists the corresponding reason-code strings/symbols that the structured ERROR log must emit and that tests must cover exhaustively.
findings.location: Implementation §4 ('raises internal PlanRelationSnapshotError with a closed reason code'); AC line: 'Each PlanRelationSnapshotError reason produces a structured ERROR log'; Verification: 'Failure-path tests cover every … PlanRelationSnapshotError reason'
scenarios: Two engineers independently implement the error handler and pick different reason strings (e.g. 'MISSING' vs 'missing_deleted'), breaking the Verification assertion that 'every reason' is covered.
A test that asserts 'each reason produces a structured ERROR log' cannot be written deterministically without knowing the reason-code vocabulary.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `Reason` enum or `Literal` to the `PlanRelationSnapshotError` definition: e.g. `Literal['missing_deleted', 'ambiguous_reference', 'reducer_error', 'canonical_id_mismatch', 'store_read_failure']`, and reference these exact values in the AC and Verification sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the sign-time HEAD comparison: when ticket_store_revision at lock time differs from the snapshot's revision, the review aborts (or retries within the stated bound) rather than signing with a stale pin set.
evidence: AC14: 'PlanRelationSnapshot carries the ticket-store revision used for bounded sign-time retry.'
Implementation: 'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' — an active behavioral contract, not just a data-carrying contract.
No AC states what happens when HEAD at lock time ≠ snapshot revision, nor what 'bounded' means (retry count, back-off, or final INDETERMINATE).
findings.finding: AC14 only verifies that PlanRelationSnapshot *carries* the ticket-store revision ('PlanRelationSnapshot carries the ticket-store revision used for bounded sign-time retry'), but the Implementation describes active retry/verification behavior at sign time using that revision; no AC covers the retry trigger, the bound on retries, or the failure mode when the revision changes.
findings.location: AC14 vs. Implementation phase story paragraph
scenarios: Implementer satisfies AC14 by storing the SHA in the snapshot but never writing the lock-time comparison logic, since no AC demands that behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling path for a HEAD-revision mismatch detected under the global lock: define whether it retries collection (with a retry limit), raises a specific PlanRelationSnapshotError reason code, or returns unsigned INDETERMINATE — and add a corresponding failure-path test.
evidence: 'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' — condition stated, outcome of condition failure absent
The PlanRelationSnapshotError reason-code list covers resolution/read failures but not a revision-mismatch at sign time
No retry policy, no error code, no INDETERMINATE path specified for the mismatch case
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the global-lock HEAD re-verification happens before signature append+commit, but does not specify the error-handling path when the revision has changed (i.e., before != after under the lock). It is unspecified whether this produces a retry, a new PlanRelationSnapshotError, an INDETERMINATE result, or a silent success with potentially stale pins.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit'
scenarios: A concurrent write modifies a child or prerequisite ticket between collection and the lock acquisition; the revision check fires but the system behavior is undefined — could silently sign with stale fingerprints if the check is only a log warning, or could crash with an unhandled exception.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'REVISION_CHANGED' reason code to PlanRelationSnapshotError; when the under-lock HEAD check fails, raise it (or retry once and then raise it). The review boundary already handles PlanRelationSnapshotError → unsigned INDETERMINATE + structured ERROR log, so the existing error path can absorb this case with minimal new code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the remediation for both revision-mismatch points (outer bracket and under-lock check): name the PlanRelationSnapshotError reason code (or retry policy with bound) so that every mismatch path resolves to either a bounded retry or a defined INDETERMINATE return rather than implementation-defined behavior.
evidence: 'brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions' — the check is named but the action on failure is absent
'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' — under-lock failure path is also unspecified

PlanRelationSnapshotError is defined for 'ticket-store-read failures' but revision mismatch is not explicitly enumerated as a reason code, so it is unclear whether callers handle it as an error or as stale-data pass-through
ABSENCE: no retry count, no named error reason for mismatch, no statement that mismatch results in INDETERMINATE
findings.finding: The plan specifies a two-part concurrency guard — an outer 'equal before/after revision' bracket around lock-free collection, plus an under-lock HEAD verification before signature append — but never defines what happens when either check detects a mismatch. Without a specified remediation (retry the collection, raise PlanRelationSnapshotError with a named reason code, or surface INDETERMINATE to the caller), the system's behavior under concurrent ticket-store mutation is implementation-defined. This leaves the shared ticket_store_revision state without a complete, owned failure path, violating the 'atomically or fail-safe with defined remediation' requirement.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision … The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.'
scenarios: A concurrent ticket-store write lands between the outer before-revision read and the collection loop; the outer bracket detects mismatch — the implementation has no specified path and may either silently use stale snapshot data or throw an unhandled exception, neither of which is reviewed-plan behavior

A concurrent write lands between collection completion and the under-lock HEAD check; the plan says verify but not what to do on failure — the review could silently proceed with a stale snapshot, producing a signed attestation over mismatched material
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'If the outer before/after revision bracket detects a change, raise PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=STORE_REVISION_CHANGED, ...) and return INDETERMINATE — no retry. If the under-lock HEAD verification fails, raise the same error; the sign path aborts without writing the manifest.' This closes both mismatch paths with a single named reason code and no partial-pin state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a verification step or named test fixture that explicitly tests pin emission with at least one non-default project enforcement configuration value (e.g., enforcement disabled), mapping it to AC 15.
evidence: Verification only lists manifest parsing, relationship collection, re-sign parity, and legacy-reader compatibility test categories — none of which explicitly vary project configuration.
ABSENCE: No test fixture or pytest command targets multi-configuration scenarios.
findings.finding: Acceptance Criterion 15 ('Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations') has no corresponding verification step that actually varies project enforcement configuration and asserts pins still appear.
findings.location: AC: 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations.' / Verification section
scenarios: A conditional code path gated on a project configuration flag silently suppresses pins in non-default configurations, but no test catches it because no test varies that flag.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Verification: 'A parametrized test exercises pin collection with project enforcement both enabled and disabled and asserts related_material is non-empty in both cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `ticket_store_revision` is the tickets-tracker git HEAD (not the code-repo HEAD that `signing.head_sha` reads), and specify concretely how the under-lock re-verification is implemented (e.g., a second `git rev-parse HEAD` on the tracker dir, called from within the `append_event` lock scope, or a separate lock on the tracker).
evidence: Plan: 'the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh. The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.'
signing.head_sha (signing.py:524) reads the MAIN repo HEAD via `git -C str(repo_root) rev-parse HEAD`, not the tickets tracker.
append_event (signing.py:590) acquires the write lock for the event append but does not re-read or check any HEAD SHA.
No existing path combines 'global sign lock' + 'tickets tracker HEAD re-check' — this is a novel mechanism not anchored to any current code.
findings.finding: The concurrency guard uses `ticket_store_revision` defined as 'the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh,' but the existing signing infrastructure's `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` reads the MAIN code-repo HEAD, not the tickets-tracker HEAD. The plan's 'under the global lock before signature append+commit' verify step appears to rely on a tickets-tracker HEAD check that has no current implementation anchor, and the signing lock (`append_event`) does not currently perform or expose a tickets-HEAD re-check. This creates a hidden ordering/atomicity assumption: the under-lock HEAD verification mechanism the plan describes does not map onto any existing locked path.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5 ('Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision…')
scenarios: Concurrent ticket edits between outside-lock snapshot collection and signing could produce pins that misrepresent the store state at sign time, defeating the freshness guarantee.
If the implementer uses signing.head_sha (code repo) instead of the tickets-tracker HEAD, the guard is wrong: the code HEAD can be identical across ticket-graph changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact existing primitive used to read the 'clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA' (e.g. `git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD`), name the new accessor explicitly, and confirm it is callable both before pin-collection and under the global write lock.
evidence: Search for 'ticket_store_revision' in src/ returned zero matches — no existing API or primitive by this name.
Search for 'store_revision' returned only cache-file hits, no production accessor.
ensure_fresh() in reads.py (line 179) performs a fetch+reconverge but does not return a revision/HEAD value — its return type is None.
The plan's concreteness ('the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh') implies a specific existing read primitive, but none is identifiable.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh' as the definition of ticket_store_revision, but no existing function, attribute, or API named ticket_store_revision (or equivalent 'tracker HEAD SHA after ensure_fresh' accessor) exists anywhere in the codebase — the search returns zero code hits for 'ticket_store_revision' or 'store_revision' in src/. The plan describes it as something to 'Expose' alongside PlanRelationSnapshot, but simultaneously states it concretely enough ('the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh') that it reads as a claim about an already-understood existing mechanism. Without a grounded pointer to the existing HEAD-reading primitive being reused, the implementation of this revision token is fully under-specified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision, concretely the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh.'
scenarios: Implementer cannot anchor the ticket_store_revision to an existing API, must invent a HEAD-reading call — the before/after equality guard in the phase story depends on this being well-defined.
If two different HEAD-reading approaches are used for 'before' and 'after' checks, the TOCTOU guard may be inconsistently implemented.
findings.suggested_fix: Point to `rebar.signing` or `rebar._store.sync` for an existing HEAD-reading call, or add a one-line `tracker_head_sha(tracker_dir) -> str` helper in `reads.py` and reference it by name in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define the prerequisite LLM criterion (its criterion ID, structured output schema/`register_contract` name, and pass assignment) within this plan, or explicitly cite the ticket/plan where it is already specified and confirm its `PlanRelationSnapshot` consumption interface.
evidence: Plan text: 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object.'
Plan text: 'Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation.'
ABSENCE: No `register_contract('prerequisite…')` call found anywhere in the codebase (searched `register_contract` across all source files).
ABSENCE: No criterion named 'prerequisite' found in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py`, `registry.py`, or `xcheck.py`.
The plan neither defines the LLM pass, output schema, nor prompt for this criterion — it is purely referenced by name.
findings.finding: The 'prerequisite criterion' that 'consumes this same [PlanRelationSnapshot] object' is referenced as an existing LLM sub-agent/criterion, but neither its structured output schema, `register_contract` entry, nor pass assignment is defined in this plan or found in the codebase. The plan says 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object' and 'Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation', treating it as a known entity, yet no such criterion appears in `passes.py`, `registry.py`, `xcheck.py`, or any `register_contract` call.
findings.location: Implementation §2 ('The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object'); Implementation §4 ('Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation')
scenarios: An implementer cannot wire `PlanRelationSnapshot` to the prerequisite criterion without knowing the criterion's identity, its pass assignment, or its output contract.

The ordering guarantee ('pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation') is meaningless if the prerequisite criterion does not exist and must be created — that creation is out of scope for this plan, creating a silent dependency gap.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either inline at least one representative pytest invocation per named category (manifest parsing, relationship collection, re-sign parity, legacy-reader compatibility) or add a concrete file path / marker reference where 'recorded per-contract' commands live.
evidence: 'Run the recorded per-contract pytest commands for manifest parsing, relationship collection, re-sign parity, and legacy-reader compatibility.'
ABSENCE: No file path, pytest marker, or command invocation is given for any of the four test categories.
findings.finding: The Verification section defers the four proving pytest commands to an external 'recorded per-contract' location without naming that location or quoting the commands. An executor cannot run the verification without first finding external documentation.
findings.location: Verification section: 'Run the recorded per-contract pytest commands for manifest parsing, relationship collection, re-sign parity, and legacy-reader compatibility.'
scenarios: An executor completing the plan cannot confirm done-ness because the proving commands are in an unspecified location.
A reviewer assessing completeness cannot check that every AC is covered without chasing external documentation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the required structured ERROR log schema (key names, format, and log level) in the Implementation section, or reference the existing convention by name so an executor can apply it without guessing.
evidence: 'the review boundary catches it before any LLM call, emits a structured ERROR log with reason and canonical/reference identity'
ABSENCE: No log schema, key names, log level, or format (JSON / key=value / prose) is specified.
findings.finding: The structured ERROR log format/schema is not specified beyond the two named fields ('reason' and 'canonical/reference identity'). An executor must choose a log structure without guidance, risking inconsistency with existing error observability conventions.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'emits a structured ERROR log with reason and canonical/reference identity'
scenarios: An executor chooses a plain-text log message when the existing codebase uses JSON structured logging, breaking log-aggregation queries.
A downstream consumer of log output cannot parse the error programmatically because key names differ across implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete schema line, e.g.: `Structured ERROR log fields: {"event": "plan_relation_snapshot_error", "reason": <reason_code>, "canonical_id": <str|null>, "reference": <str|null>} at WARNING level, consistent with <existing-log-module>.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete verification step for ACs 21 and 22, such as a schema-file diff assertion (no new fields in ticket schema files) and a CLI/MCP surface enumeration diff (e.g., `grep -rn 'click.command\|@mcp' -- src/ | diff expected_commands.txt -`).
evidence: ABSENCE: Neither AC appears in any Verification bullet.
The Verification section does not describe any schema-diff, AST-scan, or code-inspection step.
findings.finding: Acceptance Criteria 21 ('No first-order ticket field is added for pins') and 22 ('No new CLI or MCP operation is introduced') are negative/absence constraints with no described verification mechanism — no command, no code-inspection step, and no CI check.
findings.location: ACs: 'No first-order ticket field is added for pins.' / 'No new CLI or MCP operation is introduced.'
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm these constraints are met without manually inspecting the PR, and a future refactor could accidentally violate them with no automated guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the home module for `PlanRelationSnapshot` and `PlanRelationSnapshotError` (e.g., a new `plan_review/relations.py` or inline in an existing module) and enumerate all consumers' import paths.
evidence: ABSENCE: no file path given for PlanRelationSnapshot or PlanRelationSnapshotError
manifest.py has a one-way import constraint: 'must NEVER import rebar.llm.plan_review.attest' (manifest.py line 7)
attest.py re-exports manifest.py; placing PlanRelationSnapshot in attest.py would not affect the constraint but would bloat the signing module
The plan says 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object' — implying it must be importable from a criteria evaluation module too
findings.finding: The plan does not specify a home module for PlanRelationSnapshot and PlanRelationSnapshotError — two new named contracts the Implementation describes in detail. Without a declared module path, implementors must guess where to place them, risking them landing in an import-cycle-prone location (e.g., inside attest.py which already has a one-way import constraint from manifest.py).
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract containing subject_state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, prerequisite_ids, related_material, and ticket_store_revision'
scenarios: Implementor places PlanRelationSnapshot in attest.py; the prerequisite criterion module then imports from attest.py, creating a circular import if attest.py imports from the criterion module.
Implementor creates a new module but doesn't wire it into the re-export chain, breaking the import path the prerequisite criterion uses.

findings.finding: The concept of a fingerprint is named inconsistently across sections: 'material-fingerprint' in the format spec, 'narrow fingerprints' in the Implementation body, and 'related fingerprints' in AC6, all apparently referring to the same artifact.
findings.location: Implementation (format spec line; PlanRelationSnapshot section) vs AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'narrow fingerprints' and 'related fingerprints' with 'material fingerprint(s)' everywhere to match the format-spec token name <material-fingerprint>.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `build_manifest` (manifest.py) and all its call sites (sign_plan_review in attest.py, the drift-refresh re-sign path in attest.py line ~378, orchestrator.py drift_refresh) as edit targets in the Implementation section.
evidence: build_manifest signature in manifest.py (line 171-224) has no pins/related_material parameter
sign_plan_review calls build_manifest at attest.py line 109
drift_refresh path calls build_manifest at attest.py line 378
Plan names manifest.py as home for manifest_pins() parser but never names build_manifest as a changed symbol
AC: 'Passing reviews emit one deterministic pin for every direct child' — implies build_manifest must emit pin lines
findings.finding: build_manifest (manifest.py) is the sole manifest emitter and must be modified to accept and emit pin lines, but the plan does not name it as an edit target. Every caller of build_manifest — including sign_plan_review (attest.py line 109), drift_refresh (orchestrator.py line 659 area), and the drift-refresh re-sign path (attest.py line 378) — will need to pass the new pin data through. These callers are unenumerated.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear after optional verified-at-sha and before dep lines'
scenarios: Implementor adds manifest_pins() to manifest.py but forgets to extend build_manifest's signature — pin lines are never emitted despite the parser being correct.
drift_refresh re-sign path calls the old build_manifest without pins — re-signed attestations are pinless, violating AC 'Re-signing emits the same complete pin set'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'Extend build_manifest(…, *, pins: list[PinRecord] | None = None) to emit plan-material-pin lines between verified-at-sha and dep lines. Update sign_plan_review, the drift-refresh resign path, and resign_plan_review to collect and forward the PlanRelationSnapshot.related_material as pins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `attest.py` to the plan's edit targets: re-export `manifest_pins` and `ManifestFormatError` from `attest.py`'s `__all__` and import block, consistent with the re-export of all other `manifest.py` public names.
evidence: attest.py lines 29-71: explicit re-import + __all__ of every public manifest.py name
Historical pattern: manifest_deps, manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_disabled_builtins are all re-exported from attest.py
Plan says 'Callers import the exception from manifest.py' — but does not address callers who import from the attest re-export path
ABSENCE: attest.py not listed as an edit target anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: attest.py re-exports all public manifest.py symbols via an explicit __all__ and import block (attest.py lines 29-71). The plan introduces two new public manifest.py symbols (manifest_pins and ManifestFormatError) but does not name attest.py as an edit target for re-exporting them. Existing callers that import from rebar.llm.plan_review.attest will not see the new symbols without this update.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Define ManifestFormatError in manifest.py; manifest_pins() returns []…; Callers import the exception from manifest.py'
scenarios: A caller does `from rebar.llm.plan_review.attest import manifest_pins` (consistent with all existing manifest parser imports) and gets an ImportError because attest.py was not updated.
findings.suggested_fix: In Implementation, add: 'Update attest.py to import and re-export ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins from manifest.py, consistent with the existing re-export pattern for manifest_deps, manifest_regver, etc.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the existing `build_manifest` call-site tests as known-stable (i.e., confirm that `pins` defaults to no emission) OR list them as tests requiring update in the Verification section.
evidence: test_plan_review.py:657 — `m = attest.build_manifest(verdict, material='deadbeef')`
test_attestation_signing.py:293 — `plan = attest.build_manifest({'verdict': 'PASS', 'ticket_id': 't'}, material='m')`
test_attestation_invalidation.py:171 — `manifest = attest.build_manifest(verdict, material='m', regver='rv0')`
test_gate_engine_cutover.py:47-48 — two build_manifest calls asserting equality
AC: 'New readers accept legacy unpinned manifests' implies build_manifest with no pins kwarg must still produce pin-free manifests — but the tests assert on current exact output
findings.finding: Numerous existing unit tests call build_manifest directly and assert on the full list of output lines (test_plan_review.py lines 657/670/675, test_attestation_signing.py lines 293/316, test_attestation_invalidation.py line 171/178/210, test_gate_engine_cutover.py lines 47-54, test_plan_review_remediation_gate.py line 28, test_plan_review_convergence.py line 137). If build_manifest gains a pins parameter that conditionally appends new lines, these tests will need updating. The plan does not enumerate them as affected callers.
findings.location: Implementation section and Verification section
scenarios: build_manifest with pins=None produces the same output as today (backwards-compatible) — tests pass unchanged. This is the plan's intent per the legacy-compatibility AC, but it is not stated explicitly for build_manifest.
If the default is pins=[] and the emitter skips emission for empty lists, tests may still pass — but this contract is unspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Implementation: 'build_manifest gains an optional pins keyword argument defaulting to None/[]; when absent or empty, manifest output is byte-identical to the current format (preserving all existing tests).'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'archived and empty-description states qualify' as readable targets for pins, but find_inbound_relationships (_inbound.py line 103) explicitly filters out archived tickets: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue`. The PlanRelationSnapshot must either bypass this function or implement its own traversal — but the plan does not acknowledge or resolve this contradiction.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'archived and empty-description states qualify'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the Implementation section: 'PlanRelationSnapshot collects children and prerequisites directly from the subject's own compiled state (parent_id/deps) and the preloaded ticket_states_by_id, not via find_inbound_relationships, so archived-target filtering in that function is bypassed by design.'

findings.finding: resign_plan_review (resign.py) calls attest.sign_plan_review and thereby build_manifest, producing a new plan-review manifest. Under the plan's unconditional-pin-emission requirement (AC: 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations'), a re-sign via resign_plan_review would emit a manifest WITHOUT pin lines unless resign.py is also updated to build and pass a PlanRelationSnapshot. resign.py is not listed in file_impact.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Always emit pins independently of project enforcement configuration') and AC line 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations'; file_impact omits src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py
findings.suggested_fix: Either add resign.py to file_impact and describe how it obtains/passes a PlanRelationSnapshot to sign_plan_review, or add an explicit AC exemption explaining that resign produces a 'pinless refresh' manifest variant and specify how readers distinguish the two.

findings.finding: AC line 6 says 'PlanRelationSnapshot exposes complete relationship membership plus related fingerprints', but the Implementation's four named fields (subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, prerequisite_ids) include no fingerprint carrier. 'Related fingerprints' appears only in the AC and is never defined as a field, type, or computed value in the Implementation.
findings.location: AC: 'PlanRelationSnapshot exposes complete relationship membership plus related fingerprints' / Implementation: 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract containing subject state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, and prerequisite_ids'

findings.finding: The plan says 'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, or canonical-ID-mismatch targets abort' but never specifies the abort surface: no exception type is named, no review-verdict outcome is defined (e.g. does the review emit a DET-blocking result, raise an internal exception, or produce a structured error envelope?), and no fallback behavior is described if the abort itself fails.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, or canonical-ID-mismatch targets abort'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback schema-state assertion: specify whether pre-pin deployed readers can verify pin-bearing manifests (e.g., by confirming the manifest parser already tolerates unknown lines in all deployed versions), or provide a concrete back-out path (re-sign under old format, compatibility shim, or minimum-version guard) and confirm no DATA LOSS RISK on partial failure.
evidence: AC #20: 'A pre-feature reader authenticates the complete stored manifest, then ignores additive pin lines for field interpretation; it must not reconstruct or rehash a filtered manifest.' — scoped to a test fixture, not to deployed rollback behavior
ABSENCE: no plan section addresses what state pin-bearing manifests are left in if the feature is rolled back in production
ABSENCE: no back-out path (e.g., a migration to strip pins or a compatibility shim for pre-pin verifiers) is described
Signed manifests are immutable after signature; pin lines cannot be removed without re-signing, making rollback non-trivial
findings.finding: The plan introduces a persisted-format change (new `plan-material-pin:` lines embedded in cryptographically-signed DSSE manifests) but does not assert the schema/manifest state AFTER a software rollback. Because the DSSE signature covers the full manifest text, pin lines cannot be stripped post-hoc without invalidating the signature; if rolled-back (pre-pin) readers reject manifests containing unknown pin lines, all reviews signed under the new format become unverifiable. The plan's AC #20 describes a pre-feature *test fixture* that ignores pin lines, but it does not specify whether deployed older software handles them identically, nor does it describe a back-out path or confirm no data-loss risk for already-signed pin-bearing manifests.
findings.location: Implementation section ('Preserve new-reader parsing of legacy manifests with no pins') and AC #19–20
scenarios: A deployment goes bad post-pin-feature; operators roll back to the previous binary; all manifests already signed with pin lines are now presented to pre-pin verifier code that was not updated to ignore unknown lines, causing verification failures across the board
A partial rollout leaves mixed-version nodes; some nodes sign with pins, others verify without pin-tolerance, producing intermittent verification errors
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add a 'Rollback compatibility' note that either (a) cites the specific parser version at which 'unknown lines are silently ignored' was already the behaviour (making the fixture a confirmation, not a new guarantee), or (b) specifies a compensating-forward procedure: e.g., a re-sign pass that strips pins using the old signer if rollback is required, with an explicit statement that no attestation history is lost.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explain in the Implementation section why plan-material pins are encoded as plaintext manifest lines rather than as in-toto-style structured 'materials' within the existing DSSE payload (or a nested DSSE sub-attestation), given that ADR 0044/0049 explicitly ground the project in the in-toto/SLSA material-binding pattern.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — a custom plaintext manifest line
ADR 0044: 'DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard; the encoder is ~30 lines, offline, scheme-agnostic.'
ADR 0049 Prior art section: 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance produced by a trusted control plane'
ABSENCE: no mention in the plan of why in-toto link 'materials' fields or DSSE sub-attestations were not used to encode the same per-related-ticket material binding
src/rebar/attest/ package already implements DSSE envelope + in-toto-aligned signing
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom plaintext 'plan-material-pin: <role> <id> <fingerprint>' manifest-line format to record which related ticket material a review relied on — precisely the 'materials used' provenance that in-toto link attestations already model. The project's own ADR 0044 deliberately adopted DSSE/in-toto as the signing substrate ('DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard') and ADR 0049 calls out 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance' as explicit prior art for op-certs. The plan neither acknowledges this prior art for the pin encoding itself nor justifies why pins are plaintext manifest lines rather than in-toto-style structured materials inside the existing DSSE payload.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear … plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>'
scenarios: A future integrator trying to verify plan-material pins with an external in-toto or SLSA verifier finds the pins are embedded as opaque text lines rather than structured in-toto materials, requiring a bespoke parser.

The project's own DSSE verification path (ADR 0044) authenticates the outer envelope but has no awareness of the new pin semantics, so pin tampering within a valid envelope is only caught by the separate manifest_pins() parser — a split verification surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence in the Implementation section explaining why text lines in the signed manifest are the chosen vehicle for pins (e.g., reuses existing build_manifest/sign_manifest path, keeps pins inside the cryptographic boundary, backward-compatible with pre-feature verifiers).
evidence: Plan says 'without adding a ticket field or user-facing command' — negative constraint, not a positive rationale.
No sentence explains why text-line manifest pins are preferred over alternatives on codebase-alignment, blast-radius, testability, or robustness grounds.
G6 rubric: 'The plan states a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen approach (its absence is a finding)'
findings.finding: The plan specifies no positive rationale for the chosen approach (text lines in the signed manifest), only what is excluded — 'without adding a ticket field' and 'no new CLI or MCP operation.' The plan never states WHY manifest text lines (vs., e.g., a structured field in the DSSE envelope predicate or a separate signed sidecar) are the right vehicle for pins.
findings.location: Implementation section, first paragraph ('Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear…')
scenarios: An implementer choosing between placing pins in the DSSE predicate JSON vs. manifest text lines has no author-stated guidance and may make the wrong choice.
A future plan reviewer questioning why pins are not a structured field has no documented rationale to evaluate.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence after the first paragraph: 'Text lines in the signed manifest are chosen because they reuse the existing build_manifest/sign_manifest path without schema changes, keep the pin data inside the cryptographic boundary, and are backward-compatible (pre-feature verifiers authenticate the full manifest then skip unknown lines).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting (a) the PlanRelationSnapshot contract and why it bypasses find_inbound_relationships, and (b) the revision-bracket TOCTOU pattern and its invariant.
evidence: 'do not call find_inbound_relationships because its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets' — non-obvious bypass of an existing API with no referenced ADR or design doc

'The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' — a novel TOCTOU-safety pattern with no architectural documentation reference
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The novel architectural decisions introduced by this plan — the PlanRelationSnapshot internal contract, the rationale for bypassing find_inbound_relationships in favor of direct relation-entry scanning, and the revision-bracket TOCTOU pattern — are not captured in any ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc reference. Future maintainers encountering `do not call find_inbound_relationships` or the before/after revision equality check will have no documented rationale.
findings.location: Implementation section — PlanRelationSnapshot contract definition and 'do not call find_inbound_relationships' decision; 'phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions'
scenarios: A future maintainer sees the direct relation-entry scan and 'helpfully' refactors it to use find_inbound_relationships, silently breaking archived-source discovery.
A reviewer reading the revision-bracket pattern doesn't understand why the check exists or what invariant it enforces, leading to incorrect modifications.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or an AGENTS.md section titled 'Plan-review pin collection') covering: (1) why find_inbound_relationships is bypassed — its active-source filter omits archived tickets; (2) the revision-bracket pattern — outside-lock collection + under-lock HEAD re-verification before commit, and what invariant this enforces (pins reflect the ticket graph as of a single store revision).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a 'Motivation / Current Behavior' section that concretely states what the existing review flow lacks today (e.g., 'a signed review currently carries no cryptographic binding to the material of its child/prerequisite tickets, so a review could be replayed against substituted plan material without detection'), making the necessity of each mechanism traceable to a real gap.
evidence: ABSENCE: The plan contains no 'Current behavior is X, which causes Y' statement.
ABSENCE: No failing test case, no repro steps, no Expected/Actual demonstrating that unsigned/unbound reviews produce a concrete problem today.
The Goal only states the desired end-state ('Bind every newly certified plan review…') without explaining what goes wrong when reviews are NOT bound.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding a new mechanism (pin emission, PlanRelationSnapshot, manifest format extension, error paths) but never states what is wrong with the current behavior — there is no reproduction, no Expected-vs-Actual, and no named defect or gap that the current behavior causes. The reader cannot tell WHY the existing review flow is insufficient or what failure mode prompted this change.
findings.location: Goal / Implementation / Acceptance Criteria (entire plan)
scenarios: An implementer or reviewer cannot validate that the mechanism is actually needed, or scope-check that the complexity (PlanRelationSnapshotError reason codes, sign-lock integration, resign parity) is proportionate to the real gap.
A future maintainer may not understand which failure mode these pins defend against, making it harder to decide when to simplify or remove the mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Motivation' paragraph before the Implementation section, e.g.: 'Today, a signed plan-review manifest attests that a review was performed, but it does not commit to the specific content of child tickets or prerequisites at review time. This means a signed attestation can be replayed or re-used against materially different ticket states without invalidating the signature. Pin lines close this gap by binding the attestation to the material fingerprints active at signing time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the title or Goal statement to explicitly include re-signing scope (e.g., 'Emit canonical signed plan-material pins on every new and re-signed plan review') so the full change surface is visible to reviewers.
evidence: Title: 'Emit canonical signed plan-material pins on every new plan review'
Body: 'resign_plan_review collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes its related_material to sign_plan_review, so re-signing cannot mint a new pinless attestation.'
The title's qualifier 'new plan review' does not mention re-signing as in-scope.
findings.finding: The title promises 'every new plan review' but the body also modifies resign_plan_review to emit the same pin set, which is a behavioral change to an existing operation not covered by the title. While this closes a logical loophole, the scope expansion is undeclared in the headline and could mislead reviewers about what is changing.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'resign_plan_review collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes its related_material to sign_plan_review'
scenarios: A reviewer approving the plan based on the title might not realize resign_plan_review is being changed, leading to incomplete review of that code path.
Callers of resign_plan_review who depend on its current signature behavior are not explicitly called out beyond the single implementation sentence.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the title to 'Emit canonical signed plan-material pins on every new and re-signed plan review' and add one sentence to the Goal: 'Re-signing an existing review is also covered to prevent a pinless-attestation bypass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm PlanRelationSnapshot has >=3 distinct call-sites (or a third is planned imminently), OR justify collapsing it to a plain return value / local dict for the two declared consumers and passing `ticket_store_revision` separately to the sign path.
evidence: Search for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' returns zero codebase hits — no existing call-sites.
Search for 'prerequisite_ids', 'related_material', 'ticket_states_by_id' returns zero codebase hits.
Plan states only two consumers: (1) 'Build the snapshot from one compiled ticket-state preload per review' at sign time, and (2) 'The prerequisite criterion consumes this same object.'
Rule-of-Three: >=3 existing or planned call-sites required to justify a named abstraction.
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshot is a named internal dataclass with exactly two declared consumers (sign_plan_review pin emission and the prerequisite criterion), which does not meet the Rule-of-Three threshold for a non-trivial named abstraction. The plan never cites a third call-site or explains why the two consumers couldn't share a plain local dict or pass the collected fields directly.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Own an internal PlanRelationSnapshot contract containing subject_state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, prerequisite_ids, related_material, and ticket_store_revision.'
scenarios: If only sign_plan_review and one criterion consume PlanRelationSnapshot, the named dataclass adds indirection with no abstraction benefit. A change to the prerequisite criterion's needs may cause the shared contract to diverge from either consumer's actual requirements, creating accidental coupling.

If the prerequisite criterion is the sole LLM consumer, a future criterion that needs similar data must decide whether to extend PlanRelationSnapshot (scope creep) or introduce a parallel struct (fragmentation).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
